categories.name: session
categories.description: Session-level events; SESSION_INIT and SESSION_COMPLETE bookend every run.

categories.name: scan
categories.description: Filesystem traversal; one signal per directory entered, one per file considered.

tags.name: SESSION_INIT
tags.note: First signal in every run. Declares scan root, vocabulary version, and whether signals are being sunk to a file. Replays anchor relative to this.

tags.name: SCAN_STARTED
tags.note: Fired once after SESSION_INIT, before any directory is walked.

tags.name: DIR_ENTERED
tags.note: Fired for each directory the scanner enters. entry_count is the number of children (files + subdirs).

tags.name: FILE_COUNTED
tags.note: Fired for each file successfully read and counted.

tags.name: FILE_SKIPPED
tags.note: Fired when a file is skipped (binary detected, permission denied, decode error). reason is a short enum-like string.

tags.name: SCAN_COMPLETE
tags.note: Always-emit summary signal. Fired exactly once per run, after all directories are walked.

tags.name: REPORT_EMITTED
tags.note: Fired after the JSON report is written. destination is 'stdout' or an absolute path.

tags.name: SESSION_COMPLETE
tags.note: Last signal in every run. exit_code 0 for success; non-zero for partial (some files skipped) or failure.

invariants: SESSION_INIT is the first signal in any capture.
SESSION_COMPLETE is the last signal in any capture.
SCAN_COMPLETE fires exactly once per run.
FILE_COUNTED and FILE_SKIPPED are mutually exclusive per file_path within a single run.
REPORT_EMITTED.byte_count must equal the size of the bytes written to destination.
rationale: 8 tags across 3 categories. Stratified per grammar/PRINCIPLES.md commitment 5: event tags for per-file operations, summary tags for run-level aggregates, incident tags for skips. SESSION_INIT/SCAN_STARTED separation lets future versions add pre-scan validation between them without breaking replay semantics. FILE_COUNTED payload is minimal-but-complete per technique #6: bytes/lines/words/chars exactly reconstructs the per-file decision.
